howItWorks.intro: Proper diesel tank sizing ensures you never run out of fuel while avoiding an oversized, expensive tank. The calculation accounts for daily consumption, delivery schedules, and an emergency reserve buffer.
rules: Minimum capacity = daily usage × (delivery frequency + reserve days)
Standard above-ground tank sizes: 250, 500, 1,000, and 2,000 gallons
The recommended tank is the smallest standard size that meets or exceeds your minimum requirement
howItWorks.outro: Tanks should generally be filled when they reach 20-25% capacity to avoid condensation and sediment issues. Your actual usable capacity is about 90-95% of the rated tank size due to expansion space requirements.

faq.question: What standard diesel tank sizes are available?
faq.answer: Common above-ground storage tank sizes are 250, 500, 1,000, and 2,000 gallons. Smaller tanks (100-150 gallon) are available for single-vehicle operations, and larger tanks (5,000-10,000+ gallon) for large fleet operations. Underground tanks are also available but require additional permits.

faq.question: How many reserve days should I plan for?
faq.answer: 3-5 reserve days is standard for most operations. If you're in a remote area or during winter when deliveries may be delayed, plan for 5-7 days. Operations that cannot tolerate downtime (hospitals, data centers) may keep 7-14 days of reserve.

faq.question: Do I need a permit for a diesel storage tank?
faq.answer: Requirements vary by location and tank size. Generally, tanks under 1,100 gallons on a farm or construction site have fewer requirements. Tanks over 1,320 gallons total capacity on site typically require a Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) plan under EPA regulations.

faq.question: How much does a diesel storage tank cost?
faq.answer: A 250-gallon above-ground tank costs $800-1,500. A 500-gallon tank runs $1,200-2,500. A 1,000-gallon tank costs $2,000-4,000. A 2,000-gallon tank runs $4,000-8,000. Prices include the tank only — pumps, containment, and installation are additional.

faq.question: Should I go with a larger tank than calculated?
faq.answer: Going one size up is often smart. A larger tank lets you buy fuel in bigger quantities (usually at a lower per-gallon price), provides more buffer for delivery delays, and accommodates business growth. The cost difference between tank sizes is modest compared to the convenience.

faq.question: How often should I have diesel delivered?
faq.answer: Most operations schedule deliveries every 7-14 days. More frequent deliveries mean a smaller tank but higher delivery costs and more logistical overhead. Less frequent deliveries need a larger tank but often get better per-gallon pricing on larger drops.

sections.title: What Is the Diesel Tank Sizing Calculator?
sections.body: The Diesel Tank Sizing Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your daily fuel usage, number of vehicles / equipment, delivery frequency, you get instant results including minimum tank size needed, recommended standard tank, days of supply. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting minimum tank size needed right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your daily fuel usage and need to find the right minimum tank size needed. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Daily Fuel Usage (gallons per day)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your daily fuel usage — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: number of vehicles / equipment, delivery frequency, reserve / buffer days, diesel price. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your minimum tank size needed, recommended standard tank, days of supply. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your daily fuel usage before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both minimum tank size needed and recommended standard tank — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to get accurate results.
If you're unsure about your diesel price, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
